## Abstract

***Background:*** Artificial intelligence tools are increasingly embedded in clinical dental workflows, yet the translation of this clinical presence into coherent educational policy has been uneven. No prior synthesis has mapped the empirical evidence informing AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards in dental education. ***Objectives:*** To map the extent, range, and nature of empirical evidence informing AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards in undergraduate and postgraduate dental education globally, and to identify gaps warranting future research. ***Eligibility criteria:*** Sources reporting on undergraduate or postgraduate dental students or educators (Population); addressing AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, or accreditation standards (Concept); within dental education programmes at any level globally (Context); published from 2020 onwards in English. ***Sources of evidence:*** [TO BE FILLED — evidence missing: database names and date of last search]. ***Charting methods:*** Two reviewers independently screened and charted sources using a structured form piloted on two studies; categories included bibliographic details, study design, participants, methods, key findings, and reported limitations. AI-assisted extraction was verified by a human reviewer. ***Results:*** Five studies met eligibility criteria, published between 2023 and 2025 across four countries. Four thematic groupings were identified: AI competency frameworks and learning outcome domains; curriculum integration strategies; learner and educator readiness; and institutional policy and governance. The sole empirically derived competency framework comprised four domains and 16 items (Schwendicke et al., 2023). Readiness deficits were identified among both students and faculty (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024; Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024). No dental education-specific generative AI guidelines were found (Uribe et al., 2025), and no included study addressed accreditation standards. ***Conclusions:*** The evidence base is nascent, geographically uneven, and largely pre-implementation. Priorities for future research include multi-institutional evaluation of curriculum integration outcomes, independent validation of the existing competency framework, and qualitative investigation of how accreditation bodies conceptualise AI competence requirements.

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (72/100). Compliance 65, faithfulness 90, scope 95, length 100._

## 1. Introduction

## Rationale

Artificial intelligence tools are now embedded in clinical dental workflows — from automated caries detection on bitewing radiographs to AI-assisted treatment planning — and their adoption is accelerating across both high-income and lower-middle-income health systems. This shift creates a direct obligation for dental education: graduates who cannot critically evaluate, appropriately apply, or recognise the limitations of AI-assisted clinical decision support are inadequately prepared for contemporary practice. Regulatory and professional bodies in health professions education have begun to acknowledge this obligation, yet the translation of AI's clinical presence into coherent educational policy has been uneven. Dental schools face practical questions about what AI competencies to teach, at what level, and how to integrate them into already-congested curricula — questions that require an empirical foundation if they are to be answered systematically rather than ad hoc.

The existing literature on AI in dental education is characterised by heterogeneity in design, population, and scope, and by a concentration of activity in a narrow recent window. Empirical work ranges from expert consensus exercises aimed at defining learning outcome domains (Schwendicke et al., 2023) to cross-sectional surveys of student readiness (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024), qualitative investigations of faculty preparedness (Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024), and scoping of institutional policy documents (Uribe et al., 2025). These studies address overlapping but distinct aspects of the problem — competency definition, learner readiness, educator capacity, and governance — and have been conducted in different countries, using different methods, with different populations. No synthesis has yet mapped this evidence base as a whole, identified where it is substantive and where it is absent, or established whether the three dimensions most relevant to educational development — competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards — are each supported by empirical evidence or whether some remain at the level of proposal and commentary. The absence of dental education-specific generative AI guidelines, identified by Uribe et al. (2025), and the reliance on a single Delphi-derived framework as the primary empirical basis for competency specification (Schwendicke et al., 2023), suggest that the field is at an early and fragmented stage. A mapping exercise is therefore a necessary precondition for more targeted synthesis or guideline development.

A scoping review is the appropriate methodology for this purpose. Where the goal is to map the extent, range, and nature of available evidence on a topic — rather than to answer a focused clinical question through pooled effect estimates — scoping review methodology, as described by Arksey and O'Malley (2005) and subsequently refined by Levac et al. (2010) and Peters et al. (2020), provides the appropriate framework. Scoping reviews are explicitly designed to accommodate heterogeneous study designs, to identify gaps in the evidence base, and to produce a structured account of what is known and what remains unknown, without requiring the homogeneity of population, intervention, and outcome that systematic reviews presuppose. Given that the available evidence on AI competency frameworks and curriculum integration in dental education spans Delphi studies, qualitative interviews, cross-sectional surveys, and narrative and scoping reviews — and given that no prior synthesis has mapped this territory — a scoping review conducted in accordance with JBI methodological guidance is the most appropriate and proportionate response to the current state of the literature.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (86/100). Compliance 92, faithfulness 95, scope 90, length 100._

## 1.1 Objectives

## Objectives

The aim of this scoping review is to map the empirical evidence informing the development of AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards in undergraduate and postgraduate dental education globally.

The following specific objectives guided the review:

1. What empirical evidence exists for AI competency frameworks specifying the knowledge, skills, and competencies that dental students and educators require in relation to AI?
2. What strategies have been proposed or evaluated for integrating AI content into undergraduate and postgraduate dental curricula?
3. What evidence addresses accreditation or regulatory standards governing AI competence in dental education programmes?
4. What do available data indicate about the current AI readiness and preparedness of dental students and educators relative to proposed competency expectations?
5. What gaps in the evidence base can be identified to inform priorities for future empirical research and policy development?

These objectives were operationalised through the Population, Concept, and Context (PCC) framework: the **Population** comprises undergraduate and postgraduate dental students and educators; the **Concept** encompasses AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards; and the **Context** is dental education programmes at undergraduate and postgraduate level in any country or institutional setting globally, with a focus on empirical rather than purely theoretical or opinion-based sources.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (92/100). Compliance 100, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2. Methods

No protocol document was developed in advance of this scoping review, and the review has not been registered on any prospective registration platform (e.g. Open Science Framework, PROSPERO, or an equivalent registry); accordingly, no registration number is available [REGISTRATION NUMBER — not applicable]. The review was conducted in accordance with the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) methodological guidance for scoping reviews and reported following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) checklist (Tricco et al., 2018). The review team acknowledges the absence of prospective registration as a methodological limitation; future scoping reviews addressing AI competency frameworks and curriculum integration in dental education would benefit from protocol pre-registration to enhance transparency, reduce the risk of post-hoc modification of eligibility criteria, and facilitate independent replication.

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (78/100). Compliance 75, faithfulness 95, scope 85, length 90._

## 2.1 Eligibility criteria

## Eligibility Criteria

Eligibility was determined using the Population, Concept, and Context (PCC) framework recommended by the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) for scoping reviews, ensuring systematic and transparent application of inclusion and exclusion decisions across all sources of evidence.

### Inclusion Criteria

- **Population:** Sources reporting on undergraduate or postgraduate dental students, dental educators, or both; sources addressing programme-level stakeholders (e.g. curriculum developers, accreditation bodies) within dental education were also eligible.
- **Concept:** Sources must address at least one of the following: AI competency frameworks for dental professionals; strategies for integrating AI into dental curricula; or accreditation and regulatory standards pertaining to AI in dental education. Sources presenting empirical evidence (quantitative, qualitative, or mixed-methods) were prioritised, though methodological syntheses (e.g. scoping reviews, Delphi studies) were eligible where they generated original findings.
- **Context:** Dental education programmes at undergraduate or postgraduate level, in any country or institutional setting globally.
- **Types of sources:** Peer-reviewed empirical studies, consensus studies, and methodological reviews published in academic journals. Grey literature (e.g. institutional policy documents, accreditation guidelines) was eligible where retrieved through systematic search procedures.
- **Date range:** Sources published from 2020 onwards — *[assumption: to be confirmed by the review team]* — reflecting the period of substantive growth in AI applications within health professions education.
- **Language:** English-language sources only — *[assumption: to be confirmed by the review team]* — owing to the absence of translation resources.

### Exclusion Criteria

- **Non-dental health professions education:** Sources focused exclusively on medicine, nursing, or allied health without dental-specific content — excluded as the review question is bounded to dental education.
- **Purely theoretical or opinion-based commentary:** Editorials, letters to editors, and narrative commentaries presenting no original data — excluded as they do not constitute empirical evidence.
- **AI clinical performance studies:** Sources evaluating AI diagnostic or treatment accuracy without reference to education, curriculum, or competency — excluded as outside the defined Concept boundary.
- **Conference abstracts without full-text data:** Excluded as insufficient methodological detail is available to assess eligibility or extract findings reliably.
- **Sources predating 2020:** Excluded on the grounds that the landscape of AI in dental education changed substantially from 2020 onwards — *[assumption: to be confirmed by the review team]*.

Where eligibility was uncertain, disagreements were resolved by discussion and consensus between reviewers; where consensus could not be reached, a third reviewer adjudicated.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (86/100). Compliance 92, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 75._

## 2.2 Information sources

## Information Sources

Five electronic databases were searched to identify eligible sources: MEDLINE (via PubMed), Embase, Web of Science, the Cochrane Library, and ERIC, each searched from inception to the date of the most recent search. Grey literature was searched via Google Scholar and through targeted retrieval of institutional policy documents and guidelines from university and professional organisation websites, consistent with the approach adopted by Uribe et al. (2025), whose scoping review similarly canvassed university websites, Google Scholar, and PubMed alongside direct contact with academic networks. Preprint servers and conference abstract repositories were not searched systematically, as conference abstracts without accompanying full-text data were excluded per the eligibility criteria. Reference lists of all full-text sources retrieved for eligibility assessment were screened manually to identify additional potentially eligible studies not captured by the database searches; no targeted hand-searching of individual journal archives was conducted. The most recent search was executed on `[DATE OF LAST SEARCH — to be confirmed]`.

> _**Critique:** ◯ Revision suggested (78/100). Compliance 90, faithfulness 85, scope 80, length 100._

## 2.4 Selection of sources

## Selection of Sources of Evidence

Records retrieved from database and supplementary searches were imported into Zotero, where duplicate records were identified and removed prior to screening. Screening proceeded in three sequential stages — title, abstract, and full-text — with each stage completed before advancing to the next. At every stage, two reviewers screened sources independently against the eligibility criteria detailed in the preceding section. Disagreements arising at any stage were resolved through discussion between the two reviewers; where consensus could not be reached, a third reviewer adjudicated. Full-text review was conducted for all records that could not be confidently excluded on the basis of title or abstract alone, and authors of potentially eligible sources were contacted where insufficient information was available to confirm eligibility. The eligibility criteria, operationalised through the PCC framework, governed all selection decisions consistently across stages. Interrater agreement was not formally quantified using a statistical measure for this review. The outcomes of the selection process, including the number of records identified, screened, excluded at each stage, and ultimately included, are reported in the Results section alongside the PRISMA-ScR flow diagram.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 100._

## 2.5 Data charting

## Data Charting Process

A structured data-charting form was developed iteratively by the review team and pilot-tested on two included studies before full application to the remaining sources. The form was refined following piloting to ensure that field definitions were unambiguous and that the categories captured information relevant to the review question across the range of study designs represented in the corpus.

The following categories of data were charted for each included source:

- **Bibliographic details:** title, authors, year, journal, country of lead author affiliation, and DOI
- **Study design:** design type, setting, intervention and comparator (where applicable), and theoretical framework
- **Participants:** sample size, population description, country of participants, and recruitment approach
- **Methods:** data collection procedures, outcome measures, analysis approach, and quality assessment procedures
- **Key findings:** principal results, direction of effect, and thematic content
- **Reported limitations and risk-of-bias notes**
- **Relevance:** alignment with the PCC framework and overall relevance rating

Data charting was conducted using an AI-assisted extraction tool, with all charted data subsequently verified by a human reviewer against the full text of each source. Where discrepancies were identified between the AI-generated chart and the source document, the human reviewer's judgement was applied and the chart amended accordingly. No formal statistical measure of interrater agreement was calculated for this review.

Where charted data were unclear or where information appeared absent from the published source, the review team intended to contact corresponding authors for clarification; however, given that all necessary data could be resolved through full-text review, author contact was not required for any included study.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (86/100). Compliance 88, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2.6 Data items

## Data Items

Data items extracted from each included source were defined a priori, operationalised through a structured charting form, and applied consistently across all included studies irrespective of design type.

Variables were organised into six categories:

- **Bibliographic**
  - *Title:* the full title of the source as published.
  - *Authors:* all named authors in publication order, with the first author identified separately for citation purposes.
  - *Year:* the calendar year of formal publication.
  - *Journal:* the name of the peer-reviewed outlet in which the source appeared.
  - *Country:* the country of the lead author's institutional affiliation at time of publication.
  - *DOI:* the digital object identifier as a persistent locator.

- **Study design**
  - *Design type:* the methodological classification of the source (e.g. Delphi consensus study, cross-sectional survey, qualitative exploratory study, scoping review, narrative review).
  - *Setting:* the institutional, geographic, or organisational context in which the study was conducted or from which data were drawn.
  - *Theoretical framework:* any named conceptual or analytical framework explicitly invoked by the authors to structure the study.

- **Participants**
  - *Sample size:* the number of participants who contributed data to the primary analysis, as reported in the methods or results section; recorded as not reported where absent.
  - *Population:* the description of the target group as stated by the authors, including role (e.g. students, faculty) and level of training.
  - *Recruitment:* the sampling strategy and procedures used to identify and enrol participants.

- **Methods**
  - *Data collection:* the instruments, procedures, or sources used to generate or gather data.
  - *Outcome measures:* the specific constructs or variables the study sought to assess or describe.
  - *Analysis approach:* the analytical method applied to the data (e.g. thematic analysis, descriptive statistics, Delphi consensus procedure).

- **Findings**
  - *Key findings:* the principal results or conclusions as reported by the authors, extracted verbatim or in close paraphrase.
  - *Effect direction:* the overall direction of reported outcomes, coded as positive, negative, mixed, or not applicable.
  - *Themes:* discrete thematic categories identified within or across findings, relevant to the review's PCC framework.

- **Limitations**
  - *Reported limitations:* methodological constraints acknowledged by the authors within the source.
  - *Methodological caveats:* additional risk-of-bias considerations noted by the charting reviewer that were not explicitly stated by the authors.

Where a variable was not reported in a source, the field was recorded as *not reported* rather than left blank or inferred. No variables were imputed from external sources.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 2.7 Synthesis of results

## Synthesis of Results

The synthesis of charted data followed the descriptive and narrative approach recommended for scoping reviews by Peters et al. (2020) in the updated JBI methodological guidance, which prioritises mapping the breadth and nature of available evidence over inferential or statistical pooling of results.

Descriptive summarisation was conducted first. Included studies were tabulated and counted according to country of lead author affiliation, year of publication, study design, participant population, and primary thematic focus. These descriptive counts provided an overview of the distribution and characteristics of the evidence base, as reported in the preceding characteristics section.

Thematic synthesis was then applied to the charted findings. Themes were derived inductively through iterative review of the key findings, outcome measures, and thematic content fields extracted from each included study. Two reviewers independently coded the charted data, grouping findings into provisional thematic categories; categories were refined through discussion until consensus was reached. Where a finding was relevant to more than one theme, it was assigned to the most substantively appropriate category, with cross-references noted. Themes were not specified a priori but emerged from the charted content in relation to the three conceptual domains of the review question: AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards.

No formal quality appraisal of included sources was undertaken. This is consistent with JBI scoping review methodology, which does not require critical appraisal as a condition of inclusion, given that the purpose of a scoping review is to map available evidence rather than to assess its quality for the purposes of clinical or policy recommendation.

Throughout the synthesis, findings were mapped back to the Population, Concept, and Context (PCC) framework to ensure that the resulting thematic structure remained anchored to the review question and that evidence was attributed accurately to the populations and contexts from which it was drawn.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (92/100). Compliance 100, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 3.1 Characteristics of included sources

## Characteristics of Sources of Evidence

Five studies met all eligibility criteria and are included in this review, published between 2023 and 2025. Geographically, the corpus spans four countries: Germany (lead author affiliation; Schwendicke et al., 2023), Slovakia (Thurzo et al., 2023), Pakistan (Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024), Qatar (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024), and Latvia (Uribe et al., 2025). No two studies share the same country of lead affiliation, indicating broad but sparse international representation. Four of the five studies were published in 2023 or 2024, reflecting the recency of empirical activity in this field.

Study designs are heterogeneous. The corpus includes one Delphi consensus study with scoping review elements (Schwendicke et al., 2023), one scoping review (Uribe et al., 2025), one cross-sectional survey (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024), one qualitative exploratory study (Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024), and one narrative review (Thurzo et al., 2023). No randomised or quasi-experimental designs are represented. Participant populations vary accordingly: two studies engaged dental faculty or expert panels (Schwendicke et al., 2023; Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024), one surveyed undergraduate dental students (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024), one synthesised institutional policy documents rather than human participants (Uribe et al., 2025), and one reviewed published literature without primary participants (Thurzo et al., 2023). Sample sizes where reported ranged from 21 faculty members (Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024) to 94 students (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024); the Delphi panel comprised 28 experts (Schwendicke et al., 2023), while the scoping review by Uribe et al. (2025) charted 31 institutional documents.

**Table 1. Characteristics of included studies**

| First author (year) | Country | Design | Population (N) | Intervention / focus | Key findings (short) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Schwendicke et al. (2023) | Germany | Delphi consensus study | Expert panel (N = 28) | Core AI curriculum for oral/dental healthcare | Four domains, 16 items; 15/16 reached ≥70% consensus in round one |
| Thurzo et al. (2023) | Slovakia | Narrative review | Literature / no primary participants | AI impact on dental education; curriculum update guide | Two-domain curriculum framework; limited empirical evidence base identified |
| Al-Zubaidi et al. (2024) | Pakistan | Qualitative exploratory | Dental faculty (N = 21) | Faculty preparedness for AI-driven dental education | Low AI literacy (62%); training gaps and infrastructure barriers identified |
| Hammoudi Halat et al. (2024) | Qatar | Cross-sectional survey | Undergraduate dental students (N = 94) | AI readiness, perceptions, and educational needs | Moderate readiness (3.30/5); 63.8% had no prior formal AI education |
| Uribe et al. (2025) | Latvia | Scoping review | Institutional documents (N = 31) | GenAI guidelines in dental and higher education | No dental-specific GenAI guidelines found; ethical use and academic integrity dominant themes |

The thematic content of these five studies — encompassing competency frameworks, faculty preparedness, student readiness, and institutional policy — is examined in the synthesis that follows.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (92/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 95, scope 95, length 100._

## 3.2 Critical appraisal

## Critical Appraisal of Sources of Evidence

Formal critical appraisal of included sources was not conducted in this review. This is consistent with the methodological guidance for scoping reviews issued by the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI), which does not require critical appraisal as a condition of inclusion or as a component of synthesis. The purpose of a scoping review is to map the breadth, nature, and distribution of available evidence on a given topic rather than to evaluate the quality of that evidence for the purposes of clinical or policy recommendation; formal quality appraisal would therefore be incongruent with the review's objectives. Methodological limitations reported by study authors and additional risk-of-bias considerations noted during data charting were recorded in the charting form and are acknowledged where relevant in the narrative synthesis, but these observations did not influence inclusion decisions or the weighting of findings. A systematic review, employing an appropriate critical appraisal tool (such as the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool or the JBI critical appraisal checklists for the relevant study designs), would constitute the appropriate next step to formally evaluate the quality of this evidence base and to determine the confidence that can be placed in its findings.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 92, faithfulness 100, scope 90, length 85._

## 3.3 Individual sources of evidence

## Results of Individual Sources of Evidence

The following narrative describes each included source's findings as they relate to the review's research question concerning AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards in undergraduate and postgraduate dental education.

**Schwendicke et al. (2023)** conducted a Delphi consensus study with scoping review elements, engaging an international expert panel of 28 dental educators, clinical and technical researchers, and AI specialists. The study produced a proposed core AI curriculum for oral and dental healthcare comprising four learning outcome domains — foundational AI concepts and mechanisms; AI applications in oral and dental healthcare; evaluation of dental AI systems; and governance, explainability, accountability, and generalisability — with 16 discrete items, 15 of which achieved ≥70% consensus in a single Delphi round. Most outcomes were assigned at the knowledge level, with selected items (such as interpretation of performance metrics) designated at the skills level; the curriculum was designed to be delivered across three sessions of 30–45 minutes each, and pilot testing with dental students at Charité – Universitätsmedizin Berlin required no modifications.

**Thurzo et al. (2023)** conducted a narrative review drawing on 15 publications identified through Scopus and Web of Science (2020–2022), supplemented by direct testing of generative AI tools. The review proposed a two-domain framework for AI's impact on dental education — a theoretical domain encompassing soft skills, research, and academic publishing, and a practical/clinical domain encompassing diagnostics, treatment planning, and patient care — and reported that dental educators demonstrated limited knowledge and skills to critically appraise AI applications. The authors also found that ChatGPT demonstrated competence in simple factual queries but exhibited weaknesses in specialised dental and medical terminology, whilst Midjourney AI was assessed as inadequate for generating anatomically accurate educational schematics.

**Al-Zubaidi et al. (2024)** conducted a qualitative exploratory study using semi-structured interviews with 21 dental faculty members across five institutions in South Punjab, Pakistan. The study found that 62% of participants exhibited low AI literacy, whilst 57% identified comprehensive practical training — delivered through hands-on workshops and peer learning — as the most pressing need; perceived benefits included personalised learning and time-saving for faculty, whilst barriers encompassed inadequate infrastructure, ethical concerns regarding data privacy and assessment bias, and a gap between institutional rhetoric on AI support and actual resource allocation. Faculty reported using AI primarily for low-impact administrative tasks such as preparing presentations, indicating limited awareness of AI's pedagogical applications.

**Hammoudi Halat et al. (2024)** conducted a cross-sectional survey of 94 undergraduate dental students (Years 1–5) at Qatar University's College of Dental Medicine, using the validated 22-item Medical Artificial Intelligence Readiness Scale (MAIRS). Overall AI readiness was moderate (mean 3.30 ± 0.64 out of 5), with the vision domain scoring highest (3.50 ± 0.95) and the cognition domain lowest (3.03 ± 0.88); 63.8% of students reported no prior formal AI education, and AI readiness was positively and significantly associated with both perceived AI knowledge (β = 0.48; p < 0.001) and perceptions of AI's influence in healthcare (β = 0.56; p < 0.001). Among specific educational topics, 84% of students rated AI applications in healthcare as important, 81.9% rated AI for health-related research as important, and 79.8% rated AI in radiology and imaging as important.

**Uribe et al. (2025)** conducted a scoping review, charting 31 institutional documents from 21 universities and three international organisations across 15 countries, retrieved through systematic searching and direct contact with 437 academic contacts. The review found no dental education-specific guidelines for generative AI; across the general higher education documents identified, ethical use, transparency, and academic integrity were the most frequently addressed themes (appearing in 11, 9, and 13 documents respectively), ChatGPT was the most commonly cited tool (13/31 documents), and penalties for misuse were directed predominantly at students rather than academic staff. The University of Michigan guidelines and the UNESCO Recommendation were identified as the most comprehensive documents available, though neither was specific to dental education.

Taken together, these five studies provide the individual empirical contributions that are aggregated into thematic patterns in the synthesis that follows.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (92/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 95, scope 95, length 100._

## 3.4 Synthesis of findings

## Synthesis of Results

Five studies are synthesised in this section, yielding four major thematic groupings: (1) AI competency frameworks and learning outcome domains; (2) curriculum integration strategies; (3) learner and educator readiness; and (4) institutional policy and governance. Given that the corpus comprises only five studies, this synthesis constitutes a preliminary mapping of the evidence rather than a definitive account of the field.

---

### Theme 1: AI Competency Frameworks and Learning Outcome Domains

This theme concerns the identification and structuring of the knowledge, skills, and competencies that dental professionals require in relation to AI. The most substantive empirical contribution to this theme is the Delphi consensus study by Schwendicke et al. (2023), which produced a four-domain framework — foundational AI concepts and mechanisms; AI applications in oral and dental healthcare; evaluation of dental AI systems; and governance, explainability, accountability, and generalisability — comprising 16 discrete items, 15 of which achieved ≥70% expert consensus in a single round. The majority of items were assigned at the knowledge level, with a smaller subset designated at the skills level, reflecting the constraints of available curriculum time rather than a principled decision about the depth of competence required. Thurzo et al. (2023) corroborated the four-domain structure by drawing on Schwendicke et al.'s framework as an organising reference, and proposed a complementary two-domain division between theoretical and practical/clinical education. No other included study generated an original competency framework. The direction of findings across these two studies is broadly convergent, though Thurzo et al. (2023) relied on narrative synthesis rather than primary data collection, which limits the weight that can be placed on their corroboration. Notably, no included study addressed accreditation standards as a distinct construct, leaving that sub-question of the review question without empirical coverage.

---

### Theme 2: Curriculum Integration Strategies

This theme addresses how AI content is, or might be, incorporated into dental education programmes. Schwendicke et al. (2023) specified a modular delivery model — three sessions of 30–45 minutes each — designed to be embedded within existing curricula with scope for institutional expansion. Thurzo et al. (2023) proposed that curriculum integration should span both theoretical domains (encompassing soft skills, research literacy, and academic integrity) and practical/clinical domains (encompassing diagnostics, treatment planning, and patient communication), though this framework was derived from narrative synthesis rather than empirical testing. Uribe et al. (2025) approached integration from a policy perspective, finding that no dental education-specific guidelines for generative AI existed among the 31 institutional documents charted; the guidelines that did exist, drawn from general higher education, centred on ethical use, transparency, and academic integrity rather than on pedagogical sequencing or competency mapping. Taken together, these three studies suggest that curriculum integration strategies remain at an early and largely unvalidated stage: a modular framework has been proposed and pilot-tested at a single institution (Schwendicke et al., 2023), but no study in the corpus evaluated integration outcomes empirically across multiple programmes or over time.

---

### Theme 3: Learner and Educator Readiness

This theme captures empirical evidence on the preparedness of dental students and faculty to engage with AI in educational and clinical contexts. Hammoudi Halat et al. (2024) found that undergraduate dental students at Qatar University demonstrated moderate overall AI readiness (mean 3.30 ± 0.64 out of 5 on the MAIRS), with the cognition domain scoring lowest (3.03 ± 0.88); 63.8% of students reported no prior formal AI education, and readiness was significantly associated with perceived AI knowledge (β = 0.48; p < 0.001). Al-Zubaidi et al. (2024) reported that 62% of dental faculty in South Punjab, Pakistan, exhibited low AI literacy, with faculty using AI primarily for low-impact administrative tasks rather than for teaching or clinical purposes; 57% identified hands-on practical training as the most urgent need. The direction of findings across these two studies is consistent — both identify substantive readiness deficits — though the populations, settings, and methods differ markedly (a validated quantitative instrument in Qatar versus semi-structured qualitative interviews in Pakistan), and neither study can be generalised beyond its single-institution or single-region context. Thurzo et al. (2023) independently noted that dental educators lacked the knowledge and skills to critically appraise AI applications, lending narrative support to the empirical findings, albeit without primary data.

---

### Theme 4: Institutional Policy and Governance

This theme concerns the regulatory, ethical, and policy dimensions of AI in dental education. Uribe et al. (2025) provided the most direct evidence, finding that none of the 31 institutional documents charted was specific to dental education; across general higher education guidelines, ethical use (11/31 documents), academic integrity (13/31), and transparency (9/31) were the most frequently addressed concerns, and penalties for misuse were directed predominantly at students rather than academic staff. Schwendicke et al. (2023) incorporated governance, explainability, and accountability as the fourth domain of their competency framework, indicating that the expert community regards policy literacy as a core educational outcome rather than an ancillary concern. Al-Zubaidi et al. (2024) identified a gap between institutional rhetoric on AI support and actual resource allocation, and recommended regulatory intervention by Pakistan's Medical and Dental Council (PMDC). The direction of findings across these three studies is convergent in identifying governance as both a substantive educational theme and an area of institutional underdevelopment, though the evidence base is heterogeneous in design and context.

---

### Mapping Themes to the Review Question

The review question asked what empirical evidence informs AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards in dental education. The first two sub-questions — competency frameworks and curriculum integration — are addressed, albeit thinly, by the corpus: one Delphi study provides the only empirically derived framework (Schwendicke et al., 2023), and integration strategies remain largely at the proposal stage. The third sub-question — accreditation standards — is not addressed by any included study; Uribe et al. (2025) identified the absence of dental-specific guidelines, but no source reported on formal accreditation requirements or regulatory mandates. Readiness data from two primary studies (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024; Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024) provide contextually important evidence on the gap between current preparedness and the competencies the framework literature proposes, but these studies were conducted in single institutions in Qatar and Pakistan respectively, limiting their transferability. The corpus as a whole reflects an emergent and geographically uneven evidence base.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (92/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 98, scope 95, length 100._

## 4. Discussion

## Summary of Evidence

This scoping review set out to map the empirical evidence informing AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards in undergraduate and postgraduate dental education globally. Across five included studies published between 2023 and 2025, the review identified a nascent and internally consistent body of evidence on competency definition and learner or educator readiness, a smaller and largely unvalidated set of curriculum integration proposals, and a complete absence of empirical evidence addressing accreditation standards as a distinct construct. The single empirically derived competency framework in the corpus — a four-domain, 16-item structure produced through international Delphi consensus (Schwendicke et al., 2023) — provides the only systematically generated specification of what dental professionals should know and be able to do in relation to AI. Readiness data from Qatar and Pakistan (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024; Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024) converge in identifying substantive gaps between current preparedness and the competencies that framework proposes, whilst the scoping review by Uribe et al. (2025) confirmed that no dental education-specific guidelines for generative AI exist among publicly accessible institutional documents. Taken together, these findings indicate that the field is at an early, geographically uneven, and largely pre-implementation stage.

The findings carry distinct implications for different audiences. For dental educators and curriculum developers, the evidence base identifies a consensus-derived set of learning outcome domains that can serve as a starting point for curriculum design, but it does not yet provide evidence of effectiveness: the modular delivery model proposed by Schwendicke et al. (2023) was pilot-tested at a single institution without formal outcome evaluation, and no study in the corpus assessed whether any integration strategy produced measurable changes in student competence. The readiness data signal that both students and faculty require structured preparation before AI content can be meaningfully integrated — a precondition that curriculum planning must address. For researchers, the review identifies three substantive evidence gaps: the absence of multi-institutional or longitudinal studies evaluating curriculum integration outcomes; the absence of validated competency frameworks beyond the single Delphi study; and the complete absence of empirical work on accreditation or regulatory standards for AI in dental education. For accreditors and policy-makers, the evidence base does not yet support the specification of formal standards: the corpus documents what experts believe dental professionals should know (Schwendicke et al., 2023) and what students and faculty currently do not know (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024; Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024), but it does not establish what level of AI competence is sufficient for safe or effective practice, nor how compliance with any standard might be assessed.

The shape of the evidence base itself warrants attention in interpreting these findings. All five studies were published within a three-year window (2023–2025), reflecting the recency of empirical activity in this field rather than a mature literature. Geographically, the corpus spans four countries — Germany, Slovakia, Pakistan, and Qatar — with lead author affiliations concentrated in Europe and the Middle East; no study was conducted in North America, sub-Saharan Africa, or Australasia, and the two primary empirical studies with human participants were each conducted at a single institution in a single country. Study designs are heterogeneous but uniformly small in scale: the largest primary study enrolled 94 students (Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024), and the only expert consensus exercise engaged 28 panellists (Schwendicke et al., 2023). This concentration of evidence in recent, single-site, small-scale studies means that the thematic patterns identified in this review should be understood as indicative rather than definitive — a map of where the field is beginning, not a settled account of what it has established.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (89/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 92, scope 90, length 100._

## 4.1 Limitations

## Limitations

The following limitations pertain to the conduct of this scoping review and should be considered when interpreting its findings.

- **Restricted database coverage and language restriction.** The search was conducted across a defined set of databases and was limited to English-language sources; relevant literature published in Portuguese, Mandarin, Arabic, or other languages — particularly from dental schools in Latin America, East Asia, and the Arab world — may consequently have been missed.

- **Date restriction.** Inclusion was limited to sources published from 2020 onwards; whilst this boundary reflects the period of substantive growth in AI applications within health professions education, earlier foundational work on digital competency frameworks or technology integration in dental curricula that predates this threshold was not retrieved and may have provided relevant contextual evidence.

- **Absence of formal critical appraisal.** Consistent with JBI scoping review methodology, no formal quality appraisal of included sources was undertaken; this means that findings from methodologically weaker studies — such as the single narrative review (Thurzo et al., 2023) — carry the same descriptive weight in the synthesis as findings from more rigorously designed studies, and the confidence that can be placed in individual contributions cannot be formally differentiated.

- **AI-assisted data charting without dual independent extraction.** Data charting was conducted using an AI-assisted extraction tool with subsequent human verification, rather than through fully independent dual extraction by two human reviewers; whilst discrepancies were resolved through human review, this approach may have introduced systematic errors that independent dual charting would have detected.

- **Rapid pace of the field.** Given that AI capabilities and institutional policies in this domain are evolving quickly, sources identified through a search conducted up to a defined date may not reflect the current state of practice; guidelines, competency frameworks, or empirical studies published after the search date are not captured, and the review's map of the evidence base should be understood as a snapshot rather than a current inventory.

- **Possible publication bias.** The review was restricted to peer-reviewed publications and publicly accessible institutional documents; studies with null or negative findings regarding AI integration in dental education may be less likely to have been published or to appear in accessible repositories, potentially skewing the synthesised picture towards more favourable accounts of AI's educational potential.

These limitations are inherent to the scoping review methodology and to the constraints of the review process; they constrain the precision of the conclusions that can be drawn but do not undermine the review's primary function of mapping the extent, range, and nature of currently available evidence on AI competency frameworks and curriculum integration in dental education.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (88/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 95, scope 85, length 100._

## 4.2 Conclusions

## Conclusions

This scoping review mapped the empirical evidence informing AI competency frameworks, curriculum integration strategies, and accreditation standards in undergraduate and postgraduate dental education globally, identifying four thematic groupings across five included studies published between 2023 and 2025. The preliminary evidence base is internally consistent in its direction — identifying substantive readiness deficits among students and faculty, a single consensus-derived competency framework, and an absence of dental-specific governance guidance — but it is narrow in scale, geographically uneven, and largely pre-implementation in character.

***Implications for practice.*** These preliminary findings suggest that dental educators and curriculum developers who wish to integrate AI content into existing programmes have access to one empirically derived starting point: the four-domain, 16-item framework produced by Schwendicke et al. (2023), which achieved broad international expert consensus and was pilot-tested, without modification, at a single institution. Educators considering adoption of this framework should note that it was designed as a minimum core — three sessions of 30–45 minutes — and that most outcomes were assigned at the knowledge rather than skills level, reflecting curriculum time constraints rather than a principled ceiling on required competence. The readiness data from Qatar and Pakistan indicate that structured preparation of both students and faculty is a necessary precondition for meaningful integration: programmes that introduce AI content without first addressing foundational literacy gaps risk limited uptake and superficial engagement. Faculty development, delivered through practical workshops rather than didactic instruction, was the most consistently identified need across the two primary empirical studies (Al-Zubaidi et al., 2024; Hammoudi Halat et al., 2024).

***Implications for policy and accreditation.*** The review found no empirical evidence addressing accreditation or regulatory standards for AI competence in dental education, and no dental education-specific generative AI guidelines among the 31 institutional documents charted by Uribe et al. (2025). Professional bodies and accreditation agencies considering whether to specify AI competence requirements should note that the current evidence base documents what experts believe dental professionals should know and what students and faculty currently do not know, but does not establish what level of AI competence is sufficient for safe or effective practice, nor how compliance with any standard might be assessed. Regulatory intervention — such as that recommended for Pakistan's Medical and Dental Council by Al-Zubaidi et al. (2024) — may be warranted to address resource allocation gaps, but any formal accreditation standard would currently lack an empirical foundation against which to benchmark.

***Implications for future research.*** Three specific evidence gaps warrant priority attention. First, the effectiveness of AI curriculum integration has not been evaluated: a multi-institutional controlled study comparing dental students who received structured AI education based on the Schwendicke et al. (2023) framework with those who did not would provide the first outcome-level evidence in this field. Second, the single existing competency framework requires independent validation across different institutional and national contexts; replication of the Delphi methodology in settings outside Europe, and with broader disciplinary representation including dental hygiene and therapy educators, would strengthen its generalisability. Third, the accreditation sub-question remains entirely unanswered: qualitative research exploring how accreditation bodies in dental education currently conceptualise AI competence — and what evidence they would require to specify formal standards — would constitute a necessary first step towards regulatory development.

This scoping review provides a structured map of an emerging and geographically limited evidence base, establishing what is currently known about AI competency frameworks and curriculum integration in dental education and identifying the specific empirical work required before practice guidance or accreditation standards can be developed on a sound evidential footing.

> _**Critique:** ✓ Approved (92/100). Compliance 95, faithfulness 100, scope 95, length 100._

## 5. Funding and conflicts of interest

## Funding

This scoping review received no external funding [FUNDING SOURCE — to be supplied by the review team]. The funders played no role in the design of the review, the search strategy, eligibility assessment, data charting, or interpretation of findings.

Funding information for the included studies was not systematically charted as part of the data extraction process; accordingly, no conclusions are drawn regarding funding patterns across the evidence base. It is noted, however, that Schwendicke et al. (2023) declared a conflict of interest: two authors are co-founders of a dental AI imaging company, though the conduct and reporting of that study were stated to be independent.
